repo_notes.content: Audience and purpose. This repository is a worked demonstration for Databricks practitioners who want to adopt the open Agent Skills standard (agentskills.io / SKILL.md) but cannot run Claude Code on their laptops: locked-down corporate machines, data that may not leave the tenant, and no external LLM API key permitted. It answers one narrow, testable question: can the same skill folder, byte-identical to the one a Claude Code user has, run as a governed scheduled job inside Databricks? It is a demonstration with live end-to-end proof rather than a general-purpose framework, and it is scoped to that question rather than to Databricks tutorials in general.
repo_notes.author: Daniel Zivkovic

repo_notes.content: The skill and harness separation, which is the repository's central idea. The skill folders under skills/ are read-only imports, byte-identical to the versions a Claude Code user installs: a SKILL.md plus the skill's own scripts, with no wrapper, no import, no registration, and nothing Databricks-specific inside them. All platform code lives in the harness instead: src/run_skill.py holds the plumbing (input handling, guards, the reject queue, model resolution, LLM calls), src/adapters.py holds output shaping along with every Databricks-specific workaround, and databricks.yml holds the job and infrastructure config. When the platform imposes a constraint, the harness absorbs it and the skill is left untouched. tests/test_skills_contract.py enforces the separation automatically, including an assertion that no skill ships a scripts/run.py, since such a file would mean the skill had learned about its host. The skill folders are therefore artifacts carried verbatim, not project source code under active development, and that separation is the lesson the repository exists to teach.
repo_notes.author: Daniel Zivkovic

repo_notes.content: The MVP ladder and its current rung names, as defined in README.md. MVP-0 is plumbing (input document to LLM to output document, no skill involved). MVP-1 is running a real skill, document-insights, pairing deterministic metrics computed in code with sentiment and themes from the LLM, labeled by half. MVP-2 is a builder skill, branded-pptx, re-cut in pure-Python python-pptx and emitting a real .pptx on free serverless. MVP-3 is composition: skills published once to a shared volume, reused from any job, and chained so a document becomes a report and then a deck in one job. MVP-4 is running branded-pptx faithfully, with its self-correcting vision-in-the-loop QA using Node pptxgenjs and LibreOffice. MVP-0 through MVP-3 run live on Free Edition today. MVP-4 does not run here and is blocked on infrastructure rather than effort, because Free Edition is serverless-only with no Node and no system packages. These five rungs are the repository's own milestone vocabulary; composition is MVP-3 and fidelity is MVP-4, and no other milestone names or categories exist in this project.
repo_notes.author: Daniel Zivkovic

repo_notes.content: The project's settled vocabulary, defined in CONCEPTS.md. An Agent Skill is the portable capability package. The skill runner is the generic job-side harness that executes it. A published skill lives once on a Unity Catalog volume and is referenced by path rather than copied into each job; the project's term is 'published skill' rather than 'decoupled skill'. The reject queue is the store where guardrail-blocked or malformed inputs are quarantined with a written reason so the batch still succeeds; the project's term is 'reject queue' rather than the generic 'dead-letter queue', which survives only in an older filename and a few code comments. A Foundation Model API is a Databricks-hosted LLM callable by name, with no endpoint to create and no external key. This vocabulary is what the code and the docs use.
repo_notes.author: Daniel Zivkovic

repo_notes.content: Where the hard-won platform knowledge lives. docs/solutions/ holds documented patterns and gotchas with YAML frontmatter (module, tags, problem_type), and CLAUDE.md records the operational traps found by running the system live. The most reusable findings, each discovered against a real workspace: Unity Catalog volumes support sequential writes but not random access, so any zip-backed artifact (.pptx, .xlsx, .docx) fails at close() with OSError errno 5 or errno 95 when written straight to /Volumes, and must be built in memory (BytesIO) then written as finished bytes in a single pass, with the fix living in src/adapters.py rather than in the skill; dbutils.jobs.taskValues is documented notebook-only and does not exist in a spark_python_task, which is why a chained job passes a run-scoped manifest file between tasks instead; a multi-task job must be triggered with job_parameters rather than python_params, because the latter pushes identical argv into every task; and serverless runs a task with no __file__, which is why the skill path is passed explicitly via --skill-dir. These concrete, reproducible findings are the parts of the repository least likely to be found in general Databricks documentation.
repo_notes.author: Daniel Zivkovic

repo_notes.content: How correctness is established here, which shapes what claims the repository is willing to make. The test suite is split in two on purpose: tests/ is credentials-free, with every Databricks dependency faked so it runs anywhere with no workspace and no token, while scripts/e2e_*.py are live standalone suites that a bare pytest does not collect. scripts/smoke.py is the gate: it runs pytest plus every discovered e2e suite against the deployed system, so coverage cannot silently lag the feature list. pytest.ini registers the integration marker with strict_markers, which turns a mistyped marker into a collection error rather than a warning. The project's stated rule is that a pull request may say 'Closes #N' only when acceptance is proven against the deployed system, and otherwise says 'Refs #N' with the ticket left open.
repo_notes.author: Daniel Zivkovic
